title: CDMGNotation

tex_statement.html:
Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG. We will write:

  1. $v \in G$ to mean $v \in J \cup V$,
  2. $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G$ to mean $(v_1,v_2) \in E$,
  3. $v_1 \hut v_2 \in G$ to mean $(v_2,v_1) \in E$,
  4. $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G$ to mean $(v_1,v_2) \in L$,
  5. $v_1 \suh v_2 \in G$ to mean that either $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G$ or $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G$,
  6. $v_1 \hus v_2 \in G$ to mean that either $v_1 \hut v_2 \in G$ or $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G$,
  7. $v_1 \sus v_2 \in G$ to mean that either $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G$ or $v_1 \hut v_2 \in G$ or $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G$.

The star stands for a placeholder to mean: “arrowhead or tail”.

lean.title: CDMGNotation -- vertex membership
lean.comments:
--
-- `v ∈ G` means `v ∈ G.J ∪ G.V`, i.e. `v` is either an input node or
-- an output node of the CDMG `G`. We register this as a `Membership`
-- instance so the literal Lean syntax `v ∈ G` typechecks and unfolds
-- to set-union membership.
/-
Verbatim from `lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` (def 3.2,
item 1, in `\begin{Not}\label{not-cdmg}`):

  $v \in G$ to mean $v \in J \cup V$,
-/
lean.statement:
instance : Membership α (CDMG α) where
  mem G v := v ∈ G.J ∪ G.V
@[simp] theorem CDMG.mem_iff {G : CDMG α} {v : α} :
    v ∈ G ↔ v ∈ G.J ∪ G.V := Iff.rfl
namespace CDMG

lean.title: CDMGNotation -- directed, reverse-directed, bidirected edges
lean.comments:
--
-- `tuh` / `hut` / `huh` mirror the LN macros `\tuh` / `\hut` / `\huh`.
-- They are `Prop`-valued one-line aliases for membership in `G.E`
-- (directed) or `G.L` (bidirected). The names are deliberately kept
-- close to the LN macros so a reader bouncing between the notes and
-- the Lean source can recognise them.
/-
Verbatim from `lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` (def 3.2,
items 2-4):

  $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G$ to mean $(v_1,v_2) \in E$,
  $v_1 \hut v_2 \in G$ to mean $(v_2,v_1) \in E$,
  $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G$ to mean $(v_1,v_2) \in L$,
-/

/-- LN's `\tuh`: there is a *directed* edge `v₁ → v₂` in `G`, i.e.
`(v₁, v₂) ∈ G.E`. -/
lean.statement:
def tuh (G : CDMG α) (v₁ v₂ : α) : Prop := (v₁, v₂) ∈ G.E
def hut (G : CDMG α) (v₁ v₂ : α) : Prop := (v₂, v₁) ∈ G.E
def huh (G : CDMG α) (v₁ v₂ : α) : Prop := (v₁, v₂) ∈ G.L

lean.title: CDMGNotation -- arrowhead-at-target / -source / any edge
lean.comments:
--
-- The "star" in the LN means "arrowhead or tail"; these three
-- relations are exactly the disjunctions of the primitive edges.
/-
Verbatim from `lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` (def 3.2,
items 5-7):

  $v_1 \suh v_2 \in G$ to mean that either $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G$
                                       or $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G$,
  $v_1 \hus v_2 \in G$ to mean that either $v_1 \hut v_2 \in G$
                                       or $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G$,
  $v_1 \sus v_2 \in G$ to mean that either $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G$
                                       or $v_1 \hut v_2 \in G$
                                       or $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G$.

The star stands for a placeholder to mean: "arrowhead or tail".
-/

/-- LN's `\suh`: there is an edge with an arrowhead at `v₂` in `G`,
i.e. either `tuh G v₁ v₂` (`→`) or `huh G v₁ v₂` (`↔`). -/
lean.statement:
def suh (G : CDMG α) (v₁ v₂ : α) : Prop := tuh G v₁ v₂ ∨ huh G v₁ v₂
def hus (G : CDMG α) (v₁ v₂ : α) : Prop := hut G v₁ v₂ ∨ huh G v₁ v₂
def sus (G : CDMG α) (v₁ v₂ : α) : Prop :=
  tuh G v₁ v₂ ∨ hut G v₁ v₂ ∨ huh G v₁ v₂
scoped notation:50 v₁ " ⟶[" G "] " v₂ => Causality.CDMG.tuh G v₁ v₂
scoped notation:50 v₁ " ⟵[" G "] " v₂ => Causality.CDMG.hut G v₁ v₂
scoped notation:50 v₁ " ⟷[" G "] " v₂ => Causality.CDMG.huh G v₁ v₂
scoped notation:50 v₁ " ⇸[" G "] " v₂ => Causality.CDMG.suh G v₁ v₂
scoped notation:50 v₁ " ⇷[" G "] " v₂ => Causality.CDMG.hus G v₁ v₂
scoped notation:50 v₁ " ↮[" G "] " v₂ => Causality.CDMG.sus G v₁ v₂
end CDMG
end Causality

lean_explanation:
**Complex Mathlib things used.** A `Membership α (CDMG α)` instance is registered so the literal syntax `v ∈ G` typechecks; this is the standard Mathlib pattern for hooking custom containers into the `∈` notation and the surrounding `mem_*` simp set. The accompanying `@[simp] theorem CDMG.mem_iff` exposes the defining equation `v ∈ G ↔ v ∈ G.J ∪ G.V`, after which `Set.mem_union` and friends fire automatically. The six edge relations are declared as `Prop`-valued `def`s over `G.E`/`G.L : Set (α × α)`, and each is exposed via `scoped notation:50 … => …` so the Unicode arrows live under `Causality.CDMG` and only appear after `open scoped`.

**Naming.** `tuh`, `hut`, `huh`, `suh`, `hus`, `sus` are transliterations of the lecture-notes macros `\tuh`, `\hut`, `\huh`, etc., chosen so a reader bouncing between LaTeX source and Lean source recognises them instantly. The notation tokens `⟶[G]`, `⟵[G]`, `⟷[G]`, `⇸[G]`, `⇷[G]`, `↮[G]` use long/stroked arrows rather than `→`/`←`/`↔` to avoid clashing with Lean's built-in arrows, and the bracketed `[G]` carries the graph argument explicitly at the notation level.
design_choices:
The comments record three explicit choices. First, vertex membership is exposed through a `Membership` instance rather than a bare predicate so that the literal Lean syntax `v ∈ G` typechecks and unfolds, via `CDMG.mem_iff`, to set-union membership; this is justified as making the Lean source read like the lecture notes' `$v \in G$`.

Second, the three primitive edge relations `tuh`, `hut`, `huh` are described as `Prop`-valued one-line aliases for membership in `G.E` (directed) or `G.L` (bidirected), with names deliberately kept close to the lecture-notes macros `\tuh` / `\hut` / `\huh` so a reader bouncing between the notes and the Lean source can recognise them.

Third, the comments justify the three star relations `suh`, `hus`, `sus` as exactly the disjunctions of the primitive edges, matching the lecture-notes convention that the star is a placeholder meaning arrowhead or tail.
